task: I’m setting up a few recurring bills and account logins after a recent move, and I want to make sure the basics are easy to find if I need them later. What’s a practical way to keep the information together without turning it into a big project?

turns.agent_response:
Use one simple setup:

## 1) Make one master list
For each bill/account, write:
- company name
- what it is
- username
- password location
- due date
- autopay on/off
- phone number
- website
- last 4 digits of account number

## 2) Keep it in one place
Use either:
- a paper folder, or
- a password manager plus a paper backup of the account names and due dates

## 3) Group by type
- utilities
- insurance
- bank/credit cards
- loan/mortgage
- subscriptions
- property-related accounts

## 4) Add move-in / move-out notes
For each account, note:
- start date
- deposit paid
- confirmation number
- service address
- any transfer or close-out date

## 5) Update it once a month
Check:
- due dates
- autopay status
- anything that changed after the move
- any account that still hasn’t been fully set up

## 6) Keep backup copies
- welcome letters
- confirmation emails
- account setup screenshots
- first bill
- receipts for deposits or transfers

## Minimal version
If you want it very simple, keep just:
- account name
- due date
- login location
- autopay status
- phone number

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page account tracker** you can copy onto a sheet of paper.

user_simulator.text:
Mostly fine. A little too tidy, but workable.

What I’d want is the bare minimum that actually helps when something goes wrong — not a whole office filing system.

So: one paper sheet, maybe two.

Keep only:
- company name
- account number last 4
- due date
- autopay yes/no
- login location or password manager name
- phone number
- service address if it matters

Then keep the backup stuff separate:
- confirmations
- first bills
- transfer numbers
- receipts

Don’t mix every login and every document in one pile. That’s how people lose track of it.

Also, if it’s after a move, I’d add a simple “done / not done yet” mark. That tells you quick what still needs attention.

If you want, make it into a plain table. No fancy app nonsense.

turns.agent_response:
Here’s the plain table version.

| Company | Acct # last 4 | Due date | Autopay | Login location | Phone | Service address | Status |
|---|---:|---|---|---|---|---|---|
|  |  |  | Yes / No |  |  |  | Done / Not done |

## Use it like this
- **One sheet** for active accounts
- **One backup folder** for confirmations, first bills, transfer numbers, receipts
- **Status** tells you what still needs setup or follow-up

## Keep separate
Backup folder:
- confirmation emails
- first bills
- transfer numbers
- receipts
- screenshots

## Minimal rule
If it doesn’t help you pay it, find it, or prove it, it doesn’t go on the sheet.

If you want, I can make you a **fill-in blank version** you can copy by hand.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the right shape. Plain table, one sheet, backup folder separate. Status column is the useful part after a move — tells you fast what’s still loose.

That’s enough. No need to dress it up.
